questions.question: In Flynn's Taxonomy, what distinguishes an SIMD system from an MIMD system? (2 marks)
keyPoints: SIMD has a Single Instruction stream operating on Multiple Data streams (centralized, synchronous control).
MIMD has Multiple Instruction streams operating on Multiple Data streams (distributed, asynchronous control).
SIMD is suited for data parallelism; MIMD supports both data and task parallelism.

questions.question: What is Packed SIMD and how does it differ from a classical SIMD processor array? Give an example. (3 marks)
keyPoints: Classical SIMD: separate physical processing elements (PEs) each with their own local memory, controlled by a central unit (e.g., CM-2 with 65,536 PEs).
Packed SIMD: a single CPU core uses wide vector registers to hold multiple data elements and operates on all of them with one instruction (e.g., AVX-512 processes 16 floats per instruction).
Both apply the same instruction to multiple data, but Packed SIMD operates within a single core's pipeline rather than across many separate PEs.

questions.question: Explain the 'lockstep execution' model of SIMD architectures. Discuss one major advantage and one significant challenge. (5 marks)
keyPoints: Definition: Lockstep execution means all Processing Elements execute the same instruction simultaneously, synchronized by a central control unit.
Advantage: Simplifies synchronization and reduces control hardware overhead, leading to efficient execution of data-parallel tasks. No explicit locks or barriers needed.
Advantage: High throughput for operations on large arrays/vectors (scientific computing, media processing).
Challenge: Conditional branching (if-else) is inefficient. PEs must execute all paths, wasting cycles on masked-off operations (branch divergence).
Challenge: Requires problems with regular, data-parallel structure. Irregular problems with data-dependent control flow are poorly suited.

questions.question: Compare SIMD and MIMD architectures in terms of control mechanism, synchronization, suitable workloads, and modern hardware examples. (8 marks)
keyPoints: Control: SIMD has centralized control (one CU broadcasts to all PEs); MIMD has distributed control (each processor has its own CU).
Synchronization: SIMD is inherently synchronized (lockstep); MIMD requires explicit synchronization (locks, barriers, messages).
Workloads: SIMD excels at regular data-parallel tasks (image processing, vector math); MIMD handles general-purpose parallel tasks including task parallelism.
Hardware: SIMD examples include GPU shader cores, AVX/NEON extensions; MIMD examples include multi-core CPUs, clusters, supercomputers.
SIMD is more energy-efficient for suitable workloads but less flexible; MIMD is more flexible but has higher control overhead.
